wizard.title: Risk Level Orientation Guide
wizard.subtitle: Answer the following questions to get an initial assessment of the potential risk level of your AI system. The result serves as a basis for discussion with your legal counsel.
wizard.question: Question {{current}} of {{total}}
wizard.resultDescription: Based on your answers, the following preliminary classification of your AI system is indicated:
wizard.legalHint: This assessment is non-binding and does not replace legal advice. Discuss the result with your legal counsel to determine the final classification.
wizard.q1: Is your AI system used for any of the following purposes?
wizard.q1_biometric: Real-time biometric identification in public spaces
wizard.q1_manipulation: Manipulation of persons through subliminal techniques
wizard.q1_exploitation: Exploitation of vulnerabilities of specific groups
wizard.q2: In which domain does your AI system operate?
wizard.q2_justice: Administration of justice and democratic processes
wizard.q3: Does your AI system make decisions that materially affect individuals?
wizard.q3_assists: Yes, it assists humans in decision-making
wizard.q4: Does your AI system interact directly with end users?
wizard.q4_chatbot: Yes, it is a chatbot / conversational AI
wizard.q4_deepfake: Yes, it generates or manipulates images/video/audio (deepfakes)
wizard.q4_text: Yes, it generates text presented as human-written
wizard.q4_no: No, it operates in the background
wizard.q5: Does your AI system perform emotion recognition or biometric categorization?

riskLevels.unacceptableDesc: This AI system is likely to fall under the prohibited practices of the EU AI Act. Please consult your legal counsel immediately.
riskLevels.highDesc: This AI system will likely require comprehensive compliance measures. Have the final classification confirmed by your legal counsel.
riskLevels.limitedDesc: This AI system is likely subject to transparency obligations. Your legal counsel can clarify the specific scope.
riskLevels.minimalDesc: Likely no specific requirements. Voluntary codes of conduct are recommended. Confirmation by your legal counsel is advised.

modules.m1_desc: What is the EU AI Act, why does it exist, and who is affected?
modules.m2_desc: The four risk levels and their implications for your organization.
modules.m3_desc: Obligations for providers and deployers of AI systems.
modules.m4_desc: Information obligations and requirements for human control.
modules.m5_desc: Risk management system, quality management, and conformity assessment.

l1.title: What is the EU AI Act?
l1.content:
The EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) is the world's first comprehensive law regulating Artificial Intelligence. It was adopted by the European Parliament on June 13, 2024 and entered into force on August 1, 2024.

The law follows a risk-based approach: the higher the risk of an AI system to health, safety or fundamental rights, the stricter the requirements. The aim is to promote trustworthy AI in Europe while protecting citizens' fundamental rights.

The EU AI Act applies to all actors along the AI value chain: providers (who develop AI systems), deployers (who use AI systems), importers and distributors.

l2.title: Who is affected?
l2.content:
The EU AI Act affects a wide range of actors:

- Providers: Companies or individuals who develop AI systems or have them developed and place them on the market under their own name.
- Deployers: Companies or individuals who use AI systems under their own responsibility. This also includes companies that use third-party AI tools.
- Importers and distributors: Those who import or distribute AI systems from third countries into the EU.

Important: Companies outside the EU are also affected if their AI systems are used in the EU or if the results are used in the EU. The law therefore has extraterritorial effect.

l3.title: Implementation Timeline
l3.content:
The EU AI Act is being implemented in stages:

- February 2025: Bans on AI systems with unacceptable risk take effect. AI literacy obligation (Art. 4).
- August 2025: Requirements for general-purpose AI models (GPAI) apply.
- August 2026: Full application for high-risk AI systems. Conformity assessments, CE marking and EU database registration become mandatory.
- August 2027: Extended requirements for certain high-risk systems in regulated sectors.

Companies should begin preparations now to be compliant in time.

q1.question: When was the EU AI Act adopted?
q1.explanation: The EU AI Act was adopted by the European Parliament on June 13, 2024.

q2.question: What regulatory approach does the EU AI Act follow?
q2.a: All AI systems are regulated equally
q2.explanation: The EU AI Act follows a risk-based approach: the higher the risk, the stricter the requirements.

q3.question: Who is considered a 'deployer' under the EU AI Act?
q3.a: Companies that use AI systems under their own responsibility
q3.b: Only the developers of AI systems
q3.d: Only companies based in the EU
q3.explanation: Deployers are companies or individuals who use AI systems under their own responsibility.

q4.question: When do the requirements for high-risk AI systems fully apply?
q4.explanation: Full application for high-risk AI systems applies from August 2026.

q5.question: Does the EU AI Act apply to companies outside the EU?
q5.b: Yes, if their AI systems are used in the EU
q5.d: Only for exports to the EU
q5.explanation: The EU AI Act has extraterritorial effect and also applies to companies outside the EU if their AI systems are used in the EU.

l1.title: The Four Risk Levels
l1.content:
The EU AI Act divides AI systems into four risk categories:

1. Unacceptable Risk (prohibited): AI systems considered an unacceptable threat to people, such as social scoring by authorities, manipulative AI techniques or real-time biometric identification in public spaces.

2. High Risk: AI systems in sensitive areas such as biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, law enforcement or justice. These are subject to strict requirements.

3. Limited Risk: AI systems with transparency obligations, e.g. chatbots or deepfake generators. Users must be informed that they are interacting with AI.

4. Minimal Risk: Most AI applications fall into this category. No specific requirements apply, but voluntary codes of conduct are recommended.

l2.title: Examples for Each Risk Level
l2.content:
Unacceptable Risk (prohibited):
- Social scoring of citizens by public authorities
- Subliminal manipulation of human behavior
- Exploitation of vulnerabilities of specific groups
- Real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with exceptions)

High Risk:
- AI-assisted candidate selection in hiring processes
- AI for evaluating exam performance in educational institutions
- AI for creditworthiness assessment
- AI in medical diagnostics

Limited Risk:
- Chatbots and virtual assistants
- AI-generated text presented as human-written
- Deepfake technology for image and video manipulation

Minimal Risk:
- Spam filters
- AI-powered product recommendations
- AI in video games

l3.title: Classifying Your AI System
l3.content:
To correctly classify your AI system, answer the following questions:

1. Does your system fall under one of the prohibited practices under Article 5? If yes, its use is prohibited.

2. Is your system used in one of the high-risk areas listed in Annex III? These include biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration and justice.

3. Does your system make or materially influence decisions affecting individuals? This is an important factor for high-risk classification.

4. Does your system interact directly with end users? Chatbots, deepfake generators and similar systems have transparency obligations.

The Compliance Check tool in this application helps you with systematic classification.

q1.question: Which risk level requires the strictest compliance measures?
q1.explanation: High-risk AI systems are subject to the strictest requirements.

q2.question: What is an example of an AI system with unacceptable risk?
q2.explanation: Social scoring by authorities is prohibited under Article 5 of the EU AI Act.

q3.question: What risk category does a chatbot fall into?
q3.explanation: Chatbots fall under limited risk and are subject to transparency obligations.

q4.question: What characterizes minimal risk AI systems?
q4.d: No specific requirements, voluntary codes of conduct
q4.explanation: No specific regulatory requirements apply to minimal risk AI systems.

q5.question: AI for creditworthiness assessment falls under which risk level?
q5.explanation: AI for creditworthiness assessment falls under high risk.

l1.title: Provider Obligations
l1.content:
Providers of high-risk AI systems have extensive obligations:

- Risk Management System: A continuous process for identifying, assessing and mitigating risks must be established.
- Data Governance: Training data must be high quality, relevant and representative. Bias checks must be conducted.
- Technical Documentation: Comprehensive documentation of the system, how it works and its limitations.
- Transparency: Providing clear information to deployers and affected persons.
- Human Oversight: The system must be designed to enable effective human monitoring.
- Accuracy and Robustness: Demonstrated performance and resilience against errors and attacks.

l2.title: Deployer Obligations
l2.content:
Deployers of high-risk AI systems must:

- Use the system in accordance with the provider's instructions.
- Ensure that input data is relevant to the intended purpose.
- Monitor the system's functionality and inform the provider of malfunctions.
- Inform affected persons about the use of the AI system.
- Conduct a data protection impact assessment for systems making decisions about people.
- Retain automatic logging, insofar as this is within their control.

Deployers who substantially modify a high-risk system may be considered providers.

l3.title: Documentation and Reporting
l3.content:
Documentation obligations:
- Technical documentation must be prepared before placing on the market and kept up to date.
- Automatic logs must be retained for at least 6 months.
- The EU declaration of conformity must be available for 10 years.

Reporting obligations:
- Serious incidents must be reported to the relevant authority without delay.
- A serious incident exists when the AI system has caused or could have caused death or serious health damage.
- Providers must take corrective measures and inform authorities.

Violations can result in fines of up to 35 million euros or 7% of global annual turnover.

q1.question: Who must establish a risk management system for high-risk AI?
q1.explanation: Providers of high-risk AI systems are required to establish a risk management system.

q2.question: What must deployers do when an AI system malfunctions?
q2.explanation: Deployers must monitor functionality and inform the provider of malfunctions.

q3.question: How long must automatic logs be retained at minimum?
q3.explanation: Automatic logs must be retained for at least 6 months.

q4.question: How high can fines be for EU AI Act violations?
q4.d: Up to 35 million euros or 7% of annual turnover
q4.explanation: Violations can result in fines of up to 35 million euros or 7% of global annual turnover.

q5.question: When can a deployer be considered a provider?
q5.a: When using the system for over a year
q5.b: When substantially modifying a high-risk system
q5.explanation: Deployers who substantially modify a high-risk system may be considered providers.

l1.title: Information Obligations
l1.content:
The EU AI Act provides for extensive transparency obligations:

For all AI systems with limited risk:
- Users must be informed that they are interacting with an AI system.
- AI-generated or manipulated content (deepfakes) must be labeled as such.
- AI-generated text published for informational purposes must be labeled as machine-generated.

For high-risk AI systems additionally:
- Instructions for use must be clear and understandable.
- Performance metrics and known limitations must be disclosed.
- The intended purpose and foreseeable misuses must be documented.
- Provider contact details must be provided.

l2.title: Labeling AI Content
l2.content:
Labeling requirements in detail:

Chatbots and conversational AI:
- Users must be informed before or at the beginning of the interaction.
- Exception: When this is obvious from the circumstances.

Deepfakes and synthetic media:
- Content generated or manipulated by AI must be machine-readably labeled.
- The labeling must be clearly visible upon publication.

AI-generated text:
- Texts generated by AI and published for informational purposes must be labeled.
- Exception: When the text has been subject to human review and a person bears editorial responsibility.

l3.title: Human Oversight
l3.content:
Requirements for human oversight of high-risk AI:

- The system must be designed for effective human monitoring.
- Monitoring persons must understand capabilities and limitations.
- They must detect signs of anomalies and malfunctions.
- It must be possible to interrupt or shut down the system at any time.
- The monitoring person must be able to ignore or override the system's output.

Human oversight serves to avoid automation bias - the tendency to uncritically accept AI decisions.

Important: Persons entrusted with human oversight must be adequately trained (Art. 4 AI literacy).

q1.question: When must chatbot users be informed about AI interaction?
q1.c: Before or at the beginning of the interaction
q1.explanation: Users must be informed before or at the beginning of the interaction.

q2.question: What must happen with deepfake content?
q2.a: It must be machine-readably labeled as AI-generated
q2.explanation: Deepfake content must be machine-readably labeled as AI-generated.

q3.question: What does 'automation bias' mean?
q3.b: The tendency to uncritically accept AI decisions
q3.explanation: Automation bias describes the tendency to uncritically accept AI decisions.

q4.question: What ability must a monitoring person have?
q4.d: Be able to interrupt or shut down the system at any time
q4.explanation: Monitoring persons must be able to interrupt or shut down the system at any time.

q5.question: When does AI-generated text NOT need labeling?
q5.a: When subject to human review with editorial responsibility
q5.d: When generated by a public authority
q5.explanation: AI-generated text does not need labeling when subject to human review and a person bears editorial responsibility.

l1.title: Building a Risk Management System
l1.content:
A risk management system under Art. 9 EU AI Act comprises:

1. Risk Identification and Analysis: Identify all known and foreseeable risks.

2. Risk Assessment: Evaluate probability and extent of damage for each risk.

3. Risk Mitigation Measures: Develop and implement measures to reduce risks.

4. Testing: Conduct tests to verify effectiveness of mitigation measures.

5. Continuous Monitoring: The system must be maintained throughout the AI system's lifecycle.

The system must be documented and available to authorities upon request.

l2.title: Quality Management for AI
l2.content:
A quality management system under Art. 17 must cover:

- Strategy for regulatory compliance
- Procedures for design, development and testing
- Data management techniques and procedures
- Document control procedures
- Risk management procedures
- Serious incident reporting system
- Communication procedures with authorities
- Recording and retention procedures
- Resource management (including personnel qualification)
- Management accountability framework

The QMS should be integrated into existing quality management processes.

l3.title: Preparing for Conformity Assessment
l3.content:
The conformity assessment under Art. 43 comprises:

Internal Assessment (for most high-risk systems):
- Verify QMS meets requirements
- Review technical documentation
- Verify design and development compliance
- Prepare EU declaration of conformity
- Affix CE marking

Third-party Assessment (for certain systems, e.g. biometric identification):
- A notified body conducts the assessment
- Additional testing and certification required

EU Database Registration:
- Register the system before placing on the market
- Enter provider, system and conformity information

After successful assessment, the CE mark can be affixed.

q1.question: What is the first step in building a risk management system?
q1.explanation: The first step is identifying and analyzing all known and foreseeable risks.

q2.question: What does a QMS under the EU AI Act NOT include?
q2.explanation: Profit optimization strategies are not part of QMS requirements under Art. 17.

q3.question: When must a third-party conformity assessment be conducted?
q3.a: For certain systems, e.g. biometric identification
q3.explanation: For certain high-risk systems like biometric identification, a notified body must conduct the assessment.

q4.question: What must happen before placing a high-risk AI system on the market?
q4.explanation: The system must be registered in the public EU database before placing on the market.

q5.question: When must the risk management system be updated?
q5.b: Throughout the entire AI system lifecycle
q5.explanation: The risk management system must be maintained throughout the entire lifecycle.
